title: Creating Splits
subtitle: {key:Ctrl-W} {key:s} — horizontal split. {key:Ctrl-W} {key:v} — vertical.
summary: {key:Ctrl-W}{key:s} splits the current window horizontally; {key:Ctrl-W}{key:v} splits vertically. `:sp` and `:vs` do the same and can take a filename to open in the new split.

blocks.text: A *window* is a viewport. Splits give you more than one. {key:Ctrl-W} {key:s} stacks two viewports vertically (a horizontal split). {key:Ctrl-W} {key:v} puts them side by side (a vertical split).

blocks.title: Splits Show Buffers; They Don't Open Files
blocks.text:
{key:Ctrl-W}{key:s}/{key:Ctrl-W}{key:v} make a *new window showing the current buffer*. Both windows show the same file. Edit in one; the other updates immediately — same buffer.

`:sp` {file} (or `:vs` {file}) creates the split *and* edits a different file in the new window. Equivalent to: {key:Ctrl-W}{key:s} then `:e` {file}.

This distinction (window vs buffer) is the single most important thing to internalize about Vim's window model. See [`windows.buffers-vs-windows-vs-tabs`](#windows.buffers-vs-windows-vs-tabs).
`:new` creates a horizontal split with an empty buffer. `:vnew` the vertical equivalent. Useful for scratch space without creating a file.
